story:
Kent walked with his daughter, Lyric, from the parking lot toward the fairgrounds. “Stay with me, Lyre,” he said, and she took his hand. There were flags and music and a streaming crowd. She pointed at balloons, but he steered them to the ticket line first. When she asked if Mommy was inside, he sighed and told her no—today she was with him. He explained that both parents loved her and were sharing time because they were busy. Lyric nodded like she understood.

Inside, a man on stilts in a top hat bent down to give Lyric a flyer. Kent read it: a magic show later. First, they found the food court. He bought hot dogs, tucked a napkin into her shirt to catch mustard, and watched the lines thicken. She was a good kid. After lunch, Lyric asked for cotton candy—pink—and Kent agreed. They headed down the fairway toward the rides, past barkers calling out easy wins and plush prizes dangling from their booths.

Lyric stopped, staring at a huge teddy bear with a white belly and light brown fur. Kent judged the game: knock over the bottles. Looked like skill, not pure luck. The barker waved them up. Another man was already there, tossing a ball in one hand, stepping up, balking, then scattering only two of five bottles. He grumbled. Kent put down a ten. “That bear’s going home with my sweet little girl.” The man glanced at Lyric and said his name was Jack.

Jack toppled another bottle. Kent’s first throw spun only the top one. Back and forth they went, teasing, then not teasing. Their misses piled up. The bear hung above them, silent and smug. They paid for more rounds. The barker kept stacking the metal cylinders. Their throws grew harder and less careful. They shouted at themselves and stamped their feet. Jack counted what was left in his wallet. Kent, sweating, decided he needed to do this.

Meanwhile, Lyric drifted. The merry-go-round called. The magic show waited. Her cotton candy sagged, then dropped and smeared under her shoe. She tossed it out, wiped her hands, and said, “Can we go, Daddy? I’m bored.” Kent looked at her like he’d just noticed she was still there. “One more pass,” he said, forcing a smile. Jack whistled until Kent told him to shut it.

Kent wound up and finally hit the stack square on. Bottles clattered beautifully to the plywood. “Yes!” he shouted. Lyric cheered. The barker handed down the plush bear. Kent gave it to Lyric, and she hugged it with both arms, almost disappearing behind it. Jack congratulated her, eyes shiny, then asked the barker if there was another he could buy. The answer was no. More tomorrow.

Lyric watched Jack and stepped forward. “Here, Mister Jack,” she said, offering up the bear. Kent started to stop her, then let the moment run. “He’s sad,” she said softly. “It’s his… I don’t even know its name.” Jack murmured, “Billy.” Kent and Jack exchanged a look. Jack took the bear and bowed with it. “Thank you. Thank you both.” He walked away holding the bear like a child.

Kent told Lyric she didn’t have to do that. “But I did,” she said. “I needed to let him go. Jack needed him more.” Kent nodded at his daughter’s wisdom. When he asked about the rides, she offered her allowance. “You pick,” she said. He chose the roller coaster. Laughing, they ran toward it, hand in hand.
